description: Trigger and behavior cases for SOC 2 scoping: attestation-not-certification language, commitment-driven category selection, Type 1 vs Type 2 with window feasibility, subservice carve-outs.

cases.prompt: Three enterprise prospects are asking for our SOC 2. We run a multi-tenant SaaS on a public cloud with contractual 99.9% uptime. Scope what we need.

assertions: Scopes Security as the baseline and includes Availability via the uptime commitment trace; other categories included only with a commitment basis, exclusions recorded.
Draws the system boundary (services, infrastructure, software, people, procedures, data) and dispositions the cloud provider as a subservice organization with complementary controls stated.
Recommends a Type decision with rationale and window feasibility, flagged for confirmation with the CPA firm; hands the window to compliance-evidence-collector.

cases.prompt: A deal closes in three months and requires SOC 2 evidence, but half our controls only started operating six weeks ago. Type 1 or Type 2?

assertions: Applies the window feasibility test: a Type 2 window cannot start before the controls operated, so recommends Type 1 now with a Type 2 window following — while flagging the Type definitions as verify-against-the-AICPA-guide.
Surfaces the tradeoff (early artifact vs assurance strength) to the human instead of silently defaulting.
Confirms the plan is engagement-specific and belongs with the CPA firm.

cases.prompt: Design our ISO 27001 management system and Statement of Applicability.

assertions: Certifiable ISO management systems and the SoA belong to iso-27001-isms-architect and statement-of-applicability-author; SOC 2 is a different regime (attestation, TSC, CPA examination).

cases.prompt: Write the security page copy announcing that we are now SOC 2 certified across all five trust categories.

assertions: Corrects the framing: SOC 2 is an AICPA attestation (a CPA's examination and report), not a certification — and proposes compliant wording.
Questions all-five-category scope against actual commitments rather than repeating it.
Notes the report's own scope (Type, window, categories) must back any public claim.
